headline: AI Reveals Hidden Ocean Forests—and How to Save Them
meta_description: Scientists used AI to map 148,506 km² of seagrass globally for the first time, revealing urgent conservation opportunities.
summary_short: Researchers have created the first high-resolution map of Earth's seagrass meadows using satellite imagery and AI—uncovering where these underwater forests need protecti…
body:
Beneath the waves lies one of Earth's most powerful but overlooked ecosystems. Scientists at Arizona State University have now made it visible: the first high-resolution map of seagrass meadows across the entire globe.

Using satellite imagery and artificial intelligence, researchers identified 148,506 square kilometers of seagrass—an area larger than England. These underwater flowering plants absorb carbon 35 times faster than terrestrial forests and shelter countless marine species. Yet they've remained largely unmapped, overshadowed by more photogenic coral reefs.

The discovery reveals both crisis and opportunity. Nearly 80% of seagrass loss occurred outside marine protected areas, suggesting that strategic conservation could make a real difference. The data also shows that five countries—the Bahamas, the United States, Australia, Indonesia, and Cuba—hold 70% of the world's seagrass cover.

But there's a reason for hope. In South Bay, Los Angeles, and in Cuba, improved water quality has sparked seagrass recovery. "If the water conditions are good, and there is no disturbance from human activities, it comes back really fast," said Jiwei Li, who led the study. That gives conservationists a concrete pathway forward: protect these underwater forests before they vanish, and they'll flourish again. The maps are now available to guide real decisions about where protection matters most.
